title: Snorkeling With Sharks
creator: Mike Gandouin
award_or_showcase_provenance: Dronestagram (dronestagr.am) 2nd Annual International Drone Photography Contest, co-judged with National Geographic -- 1st Place winner, Nature category (results announced July 2015)
notes: MANUALLY CURATED WORKED EXAMPLE -- one exemplar only, per spec AC3.4/Scope-out (no automated scraping/bulk downloading was performed to produce this record). This exemplar is a still aerial PHOTO, not a video (the assignment for this slot explicitly permits 'video or photo'), so `platform` is 'other' (not youtube/vimeo) and `duration` is null because a still photo has no duration concept -- not because it is unknown. Verified this session by fetching the full article text of nationalgeographic.com's 2015-07-08 article 'This Is the Year's Best Drone Photography' (byline Greta Weber), whose photo-caption block reads verbatim: 'Snorkeling With Sharks -- Snorkelers swim with sharks near Moorea Island in French Polynesia. This photo won first place in the competition's nature category. Photograph by: Mike Gandouin, Dronestagram.' The article confirms this was Dronestagram's 'second annual photo competition' (i.e. the 2015 International Drone Photography Contest), co-judged by National Geographic Deputy Director of Photography Ken Geiger, National Geographic France Editor-in-Chief Jean-Pierre Vrignaud, and Dronestagram founder Eric Dupin, from over 5,000 entries. A second independent article fetched this session (weather.com/science/nature/news/best-drone-photography-dronestagram, The Weather Channel) corroborates the same photo and contest, crediting it to the Dronestagram-platform username 'Tahitiflyshoot' and listing it among the contest's overall '2nd Prize' tier (distinct from, and consistent with, NatGeo's per-category 'Nature category, 1st place' framing -- overall-prize tiers and per-category placements are different rankings within the same contest results). A web-search snippet (not independently fetched in-session due to a TLS certificate mismatch on tahitiflyshoot.com) indicates Mike Gandouin is a named principal of the Tahiti-based aerial studio 'Tahiti Fly Shoot,' which would reconcile the two bylines for the same image -- this reconciliation is treated as corroborating, not load-bearing; the primary award verification rests on National Geographic's own article text, fetched directly this session. Dronestagram's own original contest/results pages (dronestagr.am) could not be located at a specific per-photo URL in this session (the platform's contest-results pages found via search were from other contest years), so `source_url` points to the National Geographic article -- the outlet that reported and hosted this photo as part of the award announcement -- rather than to a guessed or unverified dronestagr.am permalink. `license_category` is the conservative default 'all-rights-reserved': this is syndicated editorial photography credited to an individual photographer via National Geographic, with no Creative Commons marking verified anywhere in this session; consequently `local_media_path` stays null and no image file was downloaded or persisted anywhere in this repo. `scores` (sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness) are `scores_provenance: manually_estimated` placeholder judgments based on the photo's known genre and pedigree (professional aerial photograph, National-Geographic-judged contest winner) -- for a still photo, 'motion_smoothness' is interpreted as drone-hover/capture stability (absence of motion blur) rather than in-video camera-move smoothness, consistent with `compute_composite_score`'s signature requiring a numeric (non-null) value for this signal -- NOT measurements from Capability 1's actual scoring functions, since no source image file was ever downloaded to run those functions against, per the same no-bulk-download constraint. `composite_score` IS a real computation: it was produced by calling this repo's own `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.composite.compute_composite_score` with these three manually-estimated inputs (sharpness=0.85, exposure=0.8, motion_smoothness=0.85, composition=None) and `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.weights.default_weights()` (weights_version 'default-v1', equal 1/3 weighting on sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness, composition weight fixed at 0.0), run via this project's .venv this session, which printed composite_score = 0.8333333333333334 -- so the composite arithmetic itself is genuine and reproducible, even though its inputs are estimates rather than measurements.
